proposal_text:
Proposal number: 1333885

Proposal: LET'S WALK! - climate change awareness day event

Summary


Purpose: helping to rise the climate change awareness our world so urgently needs. 

Suggested actions: During that day we encourage people to walk and to:

leave their cars at home,
skip the bus,
avoid the subway and
turn to their own bodies in order to move around the city, by walking, by using their bicycles, their rollers, their skates.
If you are not lucky enough to be living near your work space you can still collaborate by doing home office and other activities such as walking to the grocery or the club.
Using a smartphone app we intend to track how much people walk during the day of the event. This information will then be published by social media and traditional media. The data provided by the app will also be translate in the reductions already mentioned, calculating savings in relationship  with the kilometrers walked and avoiding using transportation during that day.The application may use a similar alogorithm that "calculate your ecological footprint" uses, that is available at the Government web page [14].
Because of the altruistic spirit of the cause, we expect to have support from the local ONG´s, health organizations and the government.     
Main value generation loops v1.0.png


Short term Impacts: we expect to save energy and money, GHG emissions, and we will contribute to build sustainable habits, for a healthy environment in the war against the dangers of a sedentary lifestyle.  

Long term impacts:

Since the proposal is to make people aware of how important it is to care about the planet. More climate change aware people would naturally support the following initiatives:

Recycling: We forecast that it could increase by 10 %,
 
Energy savings: recommendations use increment by 10 %
 
Distributed solar photovoltaic electricity law: is approved and covers 5 % of city demand.
 
Electric buses: their use is implemented in 2 % of city bus lines.
Is this proposal for a practice or a project?
Practice

What actions do you propose?
a) The main thrust of “Let’s Walk” is walking, because Buenos Aires has a high population density, and distances might be shorter than in other cities, so walking to work is a real possibility for some people. People can

Walk 10 - 15 blocks to their offices or back home.

Use public transportation, including buses, underground / subways and then walk the rest of the way to their offices or homes.

 

b) Cycling also adds value to the “Let’s walk” proposal for the population 10 to 30 blocks from their office. The Buenos Aires City already has implemented an “ecobici” (eco bikes) program [15]. The ecobici system has bikes parked in several distributed city points, and anyone can use one of these bikes for free using his / her document. You just return the bike in another bike parking place near your destination.

 

REVIEW OF SOME “BIKE TO WORK” PROGRAMS IN CANADA AND USA:

USA Today informs that Bike to Work programs might be rising in US [21].

Through a google search we found the following relevant links:

table-bike-to-work-projects.png


ANSWERS TO SOME OBJECTIONS TO “BIKE TO WORK”

The Huffpost article [16] mentions 9 objections to “Bike to work”, here are our thoughts about them!

main-objections.png

“LET’S WALK BUSINESS MODEL CANVAS”

LET`S WALK PROJECT BUSINESS CANVAS v2.0.png

As we know we have different partners in this event, which play a fundamental role.

On one side, the people enrolled in the event, that will participate registering using a mobile app. They will either walk to their works or commute by bike. In order to be eligible to receive a prize, each participant will allow the app to register information about distances walked / biked, which transportation they usually use to commute in order to calculate GHG emission savings due to the event. Those people that contribute with the biggest GHG emission savings will win prizes as well as  benefits from companies that sponsor the event.

It´s necessary to distinguish, that by incentivizing the movement we are preventing obesity, decrease bad cholesterol and increase good cholesterol, improving the state of mind and incorporating healthy habits, so typically HMOs and the city government will be very much interested, as in Argentina poor people use public hospitals maintained by city taxes.

On the other side, we need to be able to organize this event with which we need the Government's support to convey the event to the society. It will be benefited by an increased use of the public transport by the population, better flow of the traffic (less cars in the streets)  and at the same time it will reaffirm it´s commitment with the environment.

In addition to this, it will be a great opportunity to advertise the use of the eco-bikes(“ecobicis”) [15] and, as a result, the bike lanes.

On the other hand, the information generated by the app (big data)  will be of great utility since it will show which are the population's needs (for example: the best locations for entrances of subways , routes of buses or even which are the areas that are disconnected and need public transport).

It is also important to have the companies that will join on this great event allowing the use of “home office”. In addition, they will collaborate as sponsors and obtain in exchange Big Data to improve its business and find niche.

Sponsorships are one of the best ways of promoting a business and winning popularity between the target and generate good marketing for a brand. It can be specially used to increase credibility, image and prestige of the brand.

All participation is voluntary, and public transportation will still be available to those who want to use it.

LGC Team will ask for suggestions and advice from NGOs(such as WWF, who implemented "The earth hour"). In order to have more tools to guide the team on how to develop the project. 
 
Once LCG Team recieves feedback from NGOs.They will present the formal proposal to the Institute of Technology of Buenos Aires (ITBA) and to the MIT Colab
 
The team is looking forward to receive feedback from CLIMATE COLAB in order to improve the project. ITBA will give their consent and support to it.
 
We received the recognition and prize from MIT Colab thanks to the backing through votes from ITBA.
 
LGC Team will contact several organizations to help with fundraising and to add value to the final event (GOOGLE, MASS MEDIA, SPONSORS, etc). Nevertheless to make it happen we need firstly the GOVERNMENT authorization.
 
The government will notificate the companies of the proposal and ask for their cooperation by allowing the employees to work from home. They will also have to arrange with the transportation about the modification of their routes.
 
Distance walked and GHG savings: To have an estimation of the GHGs saved is necessary an app (develop by GOOGLE if possible) that will measure them.The application may use a similar alogorithm that "calculate your ecological footprint" uses, that is available at the Government web page.But it is important to keep record of the route like Google Maps does. 
 
Once the proposal is accepted the next step is to settle a date and communicate it to the society, a key actor in this process is MASS MEDIA. 
 
The day of the event SPONSORS will play an important role by motivating the population to walk more. This could be achived by offering refreshments and prizes. 
 
Finally,all the information is collected by GOOGLE. Including steps per person and the equivalence with the reduction of GHGs obteined.
 
The LGC Team will analyze the results and make papers to be published and to communicate it to the community, the government and the MIT. 
 
As the results will be positive this idea can be implemented in other countries, all over the world. By this the proposal will reach a greater impact and a change in people's habits worldwide.


Who will take these actions?
LGC Team at ITBA: designs proposal and presents it to Climate Colab.
 
Argentine Government sponsors "LET'S WALK day"
 
Media covers the events publishing metrics on energy saved, emissions saved and money saved.
 
5 % local city population participates in the "LET'S WALK day"
 
NGOs sponsor event.
 
Transportation works as usual.

Other countries can implement the proposal to have a higher impact
Where will these actions be taken?
City level: The first objective is to do it in Buenos Aires City as is the most populated region, the most contaminating one and the principal concentration of companies.

Country level: Later by the positive results our goal is to carry out the experience at country level, in cities like Cordoba, Rosario, Mendoza and San Luis.

Worldwide: Finally, other countries can use the proposal to make their own impacts since everything counts.

 

In addition, specify the country or countries where these actions will be taken.
Argentina

Country 2
Brazil

Country 3
Chile

Country 4
United States

Country 5
No country selected

Impact/Benefits
What impact will these actions have on greenhouse gas emissions and/or adapting to climate change?
1) City of Buenos Aires

a) Short term CO2e emissions saved in Buenos Aires city:

We postulate that 5 % of the population will participate in the event.
Public transportation use: 80% of population [1]

 
Emissions saved by cars commuting from Buenos Aires Province:

10410352 cars/month [2] /30 days/month x 15 km/trip  x 0.1 kg CO2eq / km [3] x 0.05 ( 5 % population participates  in event) = 26 tCO2e (metric tons) / day.
 

Emission saved by cars inside the city:

2.89 million people x 5% people participation x 20 % people use cars x 7 km average trip to work x 2 trips /day x 0.1 kg CO2eq / km [3] = 40.46 tCO2e / day
 

Total short term CO2e savings = (26 + 40.46) = 66.46 tCO2e /day

b) Long term CHG savings (5 years) in Buenos Aires city:

We forecast that recycling could increase by 10 %. Today only 23% of Buenos Aires city recycle [4]. And recycling saves from 0.34 to 10 Tonnes of CO2e from each ton of recycled material [5]. Considering an average 1 Tonne of CO2e saved per Tonne of recycled material, current Buenos Aires savings from recycling would be 976 Tonnes of domiciliary residues [6] x 23% people recycle x 0.1 (10 % recycling increase ) x 1 tCO2e/1 t recycled material = 22 Tonnes of CO2e / year saved.
 
Energy savings recommendations [7] use increment by 10 %. The average electricity consumption per electrified household in Argentina is 3600 kWh [11], so we estimate that the savings will be of 360 kWh/home per year * 963 thousand homes * 0.05 (5 % of participation) = 17334 kWh per year. This could save up to 17334 kWh x 0.0005925 tCO2e/kWh = 10.2 tCO2e/year
Distributed solar photo voltaic electricity law is approved and covers 1 % of city demand.
2.89 million people x 4100 kWh/person [10] x 0.01 (1% of demand) x 0.0005925 tCO2e/kWh = 70205 tCO2e/year
 
It incentivates the use of public transport in this way, so the Government must make it more efficient.For example, the use of electric buses is implemented in 2 % of city.[8] [9] Each bus will save 175 metrics tones of pollutant emissions and 22.700 liters of fuel per unit and year. We estimate that there are 10200 buses in Buenos Aires,so 2% is 204, the total reduction will be 204*175 mtones= 35700 metric tones of pollutant / year saved.[10]
 
Total emission savings = (22 + 10.2 + 70205 + 35700) = 105937 tCO2e/year

2) Extrapolation to world:

a) Short term:

66.46 tCO2e/(2.89million people) x (1087 million people) = 24997 tCO2e

b) Long term:

105.937 tCO2e/year /(2.89million people) x (1087 million people) = 39 million tCO2e / year.

The transportation sector accounts from 19 % to 25 % of total GHGs emissions, according to [12] and [13]

So the total GHG due to the transportation sector may be calculated as

46.000 million tCO2e x 22% = 10.120 million tCO2e

So the proposal savings would be 39 / 10120 = 0.38 % of the transportation sector emissions.

What are other key benefits?
Generate awareness on using more public transport
Government will have to improve the efficiency of public transport
Change the habits of the society towards a sustainable and ecological life
Smog and sound pollution will be reduced
People´s stress levels will be reduced
Increase the use of bicycles
Change habits and weaken sedentary lifestyle
Cut costs of transport and so combustibles
By implemeting home-office besides helping this cause, the employeers will have a cut down of their costs
Decrease GHGs emissions by reducing the use of cars and public transport
Costs/Challenges
What are the proposal’s projected costs?
1) Cost of security forces involved in organizing the event.

2) Cost of health support personnel, ambulances, paramedics,

3) Cost of traditional media ads

4) Cost of blog, SN ads and  social media managers / influencers

5) Cost of winner prizes

6) Cost of collaboration agreement with smartphone apps that calculate distances walked by people participating in the event

7) Cost of webpage that summarizes energy savings, money saved, GHGs emission saved.

Timeline
The day of the event, there will be an immediate impact by the reduction of the GHGs emissions.
In the short-term we are looking forward to make people realize that their can make a huge change on a daily basis.For example, they can avoid the use of cars in short distances.
Over more, we can affirm that one of important change will be the use bikes. Nowadays, Buenos Aires city provides free bikes and bicicles in the downtown. Our idea is to massify these kind of habits and became a green country free of sedentarism.
What's more, we want to Incentive companies to stablish a home-office day. This will allow people who live far from their jobs to colaborate to this cause.
Medium-term: These "Homeoffice days" could at first be once a year, but once people catch the habit, it could be done monthly.
Long-term: Eventually people could do these "Homeoffice days" weekly, so that people enjoy life a little more and savings of energy, money and GHGs emissions are multiplied.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a highly optimistic and unverified assumption that 5% of the city's population (approx. 145,000 people) will voluntarily participate in a single-day event without significant logistical friction. The technical requirement for a custom app to track routes and calculate GHG savings in real-time, while citing existing algorithms, lacks a defined development or integration pathway. Furthermore, the causal chain assumes that a one-day behavioral change will trigger complex, multi-year policy shifts (e.g., 2% electric bus fleet conversion, solar law approval) with high certainty, ignoring the long lead times and political inertia inherent in infrastructure and legislative systems.

novelty.rationale: The core concept—a 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' day—is a well-established practice globally, with the proposal explicitly citing similar programs in the US and Canada. The addition of a smartphone app for tracking and gamification is a common feature in existing mobility apps (e.g., Strava, Google Maps) and does not represent a novel technological or systemic innovation. The proposal repackages existing awareness campaigns rather than introducing a new mechanism for behavior change or emission reduction.

impact.rationale: The short-term impact calculation (66.46 tCO2e/day) is mathematically plausible based on the stated participation rate, though the actual reduction is likely negligible on a global scale. However, the long-term impact projections are scientifically unsound. The proposal attributes massive systemic changes (e.g., 70,205 tCO2e/year from solar law approval and 35,700 tCO2e/year from electric bus implementation) directly to a single awareness event. This represents a severe attribution error; these outcomes depend on independent legislative and capital investment cycles, not the event itself. The extrapolation to a global scale (39 million tCO2e/year) compounds these errors by assuming uniform adoption and causal efficacy across diverse geopolitical contexts.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts to provide quantitative backing for its claims. However, the presentation suffers from significant technical inconsistencies, including typos ('kilometrers', 'aligorithm'), broken image references ('Main value generation loops v1.0.png'), and a lack of clarity in the causal logic. The data sources are cited but the derivation of the long-term impact figures is opaque and appears to conflate correlation with causation, reducing the scientific credibility of the presentation.

A1.perspective_summary: From a systems and empirical evidence perspective, this proposal functions more as a symbolic awareness campaign than a rigorous climate intervention. While the short-term behavioral shift is theoretically possible, the causal mechanisms linking a one-day event to long-term infrastructure and legislative outcomes are tenuous and lack empirical support. The quantitative impact model contains significant attribution errors, inflating the projected efficacy by assuming the event is the primary driver of complex, multi-year systemic changes.
key_concerns: Causal Attribution Error: The proposal incorrectly attributes long-term policy outcomes (solar law, electric bus fleet conversion) to a single-day awareness event, ignoring the independent variables of political will, budget cycles, and infrastructure planning.
Participation Uncertainty: The feasibility model hinges on a 5% voluntary participation rate without a defined mobilization strategy or historical baseline for similar events in Buenos Aires.

Technical Implementation Gap: The proposal assumes the immediate availability of a custom app with specific GHG calculation algorithms and route tracking, without addressing development costs, data privacy, or integration with existing municipal systems.

Extrapolation Validity: The global impact projection assumes that a localized event in Buenos Aires can be linearly scaled to 1087 million people with identical behavioral and systemic responses, which is scientifically implausible.

Data Integrity: The reliance on broken image links and undefined data sources for the 'Business Model Canvas' and 'Value Generation Loops' undermines the ability to verify the proposed system dynamics.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on unrealistic institutional assumptions, specifically that the government will 'arrange with transportation about the modification of their routes' for a single-day event and that companies will mandate home office based on a voluntary awareness day. The governance capacity required to coordinate security, health, media, and transport authorities for a one-off event is not addressed, and the reliance on a 'Google-developed' app without a clear procurement or partnership framework presents a significant regulatory and operational gap.

novelty.rationale: The concept of 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days is a well-established global practice (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work Day). The proposal's novelty lies primarily in the specific integration of a tracking app and the attempt to link short-term behavioral data to long-term policy outcomes (e.g., solar law approval), but the core mechanism is not institutionally novel.

impact.rationale: The projected long-term impacts (e.g., 10% increase in recycling, 5% city demand covered by solar) are causally disconnected from a single-day walking event. The proposal assumes a 'trickle-down' effect where awareness automatically translates into complex legislative and infrastructural changes without detailing the policy advocacy or institutional levers required to achieve them. The short-term emission savings are real but negligible at a global scale.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts to use data, but it suffers from significant logical gaps in its impact modeling and governance assumptions. The text references image files (e.g., 'Main value generation loops v1.0.png') that are not visible, and the calculation logic for long-term impacts appears to conflate correlation with causation, weakening the persuasive power of the argument.

A2.perspective_summary: From a governance perspective, this proposal is a classic 'awareness campaign' that overestimates the ability of a single event to drive systemic policy change. While the intent to reduce emissions and improve public health is sound, the institutional feasibility is low due to the lack of a concrete implementation plan for government coordination and the unrealistic expectation that a one-day event will trigger complex legislative outcomes like solar law adoption. The proposal needs a stronger focus on the specific regulatory and political mechanisms required to sustain the behavior change beyond the event day.
key_concerns: The assumption that the government will modify public transport routes for a single-day event is operationally and politically unrealistic without a long-term strategic plan.
There is no clear governance framework for the proposed 'Google-developed' app, including data privacy, liability, and procurement processes.
The causal link between a one-day walking event and long-term policy outcomes (e.g., 5% solar coverage, 10% recycling increase) is not supported by a policy advocacy strategy.
The proposal lacks a risk management plan for public safety and crowd control, which are critical for government approval of mass participation events.
The reliance on voluntary corporate participation for home office days ignores labor laws and the need for formal agreements or incentives.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'big bang' single-day event model with unrealistic dependencies: government authorization to modify bus routes, Google developing a custom app, and 5% of the city's population participating simultaneously. The operational complexity of coordinating security, health support, and mass media for a one-off event without a proven pilot or dedicated budget is extremely high. The assumption that the government will alter public transport routes for a single day is operationally infeasible.

novelty.rationale: The core concept—'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days—is a well-established global practice (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work Month). The proposal adds a smartphone tracking component, but this is a standard feature in existing fitness and carbon footprint apps. The novelty lies only in the specific local context of Buenos Aires, not in the mechanism itself.

impact.rationale: While the short-term emission savings (66 tCO2e/day) are mathematically derived, they are negligible on a global scale and likely overestimated due to the assumption of 5% participation without a robust incentive structure. The long-term impact projections (e.g., 39 million tCO2e/year globally) are speculative extrapolations based on a single unproven event. The proposal conflates a one-day behavioral nudge with systemic infrastructure changes (like electric bus adoption) that require years of policy work, not a single event.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts to use data, but the logic is often circular or based on unverified assumptions (e.g., assuming the government will approve the event before the event is fully planned). The inclusion of a Business Model Canvas and references to existing programs shows effort, but the text contains grammatical errors and the financial model is vague, listing costs without identifying a clear payer or revenue stream beyond 'sponsorships'.

A3.perspective_summary: From an implementation standpoint, this proposal is a high-risk, low-feasibility event plan that mistakes a one-day awareness campaign for a scalable sustainability solution. The operational barriers—specifically the need for government route changes and a custom app from a major tech giant—are significant blockers. The financial model is undefined, relying entirely on voluntary sponsorships for a high-cost event (security, health, media). While the intent is positive, the execution plan lacks the pragmatic steps required for real-world deployment.
key_concerns: Who pays for the high operational costs (security, ambulances, media) if sponsorships fail to materialize?
The assumption that the government will modify public transport routes for a single day is operationally unrealistic.
Reliance on Google to develop a custom tracking app is a critical single point of failure with no fallback plan.
The 5% participation rate is an optimistic assumption without a proven incentive mechanism or marketing budget.
The long-term impact projections (e.g., 39 million tCO2e) are mathematically unsound extrapolations from a single-day event.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on unrealistic assumptions about government and corporate behavior, specifically that the government will 'arrange with transportation about the modification of their routes' and that companies will universally allow home office for a single event without prior negotiation. The requirement for a custom Google-developed app to track specific routes and calculate GHG savings creates a high technical and bureaucratic barrier that ignores existing, accessible tools (like Strava or Google Fit) that could be leveraged immediately.

novelty.rationale: The core concept of a 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' day is a well-established global practice, not a novel innovation. While the proposal attempts to add value through 'big data' collection and specific GHG calculations, the fundamental mechanism is a standard awareness campaign. The novelty is further diminished by the reliance on existing 'Ecobici' infrastructure and standard corporate sponsorship models.

impact.rationale: The short-term impact of a single day of walking is negligible in the grand scheme of climate change (66 tCO2e/day is a drop in the bucket for a city of 3 million). However, the proposal correctly identifies potential long-term behavioral shifts (reduced sedentary lifestyle, increased public transport use). The impact is heavily contingent on the 'awareness' translating into permanent habit change, which is historically difficult to achieve with one-off events. The health benefits for participants are a strong, tangible positive.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections but suffers from significant clarity issues regarding the 'business model' and data flow. The text oscillates between asking for government route modifications and suggesting home office, creating a confusing narrative about the primary action. The reliance on image placeholders (e.g., 'main-objections.png') without describing the content in the text makes the argument incomplete. The tone is optimistic but lacks the grounded detail needed to convince stakeholders of practical execution.

A4.perspective_summary: From the ground, this proposal feels like a top-down awareness campaign that underestimates the daily friction of commuting in a dense city like Buenos Aires. While the health and community spirit aspects are valuable, the plan assumes a level of institutional cooperation (route changes, universal home office) that rarely happens without deep, pre-existing relationships. The 'big data' angle feels like a solution looking for a problem, potentially alienating participants who just want to walk without being tracked by a custom app.
key_concerns: The proposal assumes the government will modify public transport routes for a one-day event, which is operationally unlikely and could disrupt regular commuters.
Relying on a custom app developed by Google for tracking creates a barrier to entry; many residents may not have smartphones or trust the data privacy implications.
The calculation of long-term impacts (e.g., 39 million tCO2e/year globally) is based on highly speculative extrapolations of a single-day event's success.
The proposal does not address accessibility for people with mobility impairments, the elderly, or those with caregiving responsibilities who cannot 'just walk' to work.
There is a risk of 'performative activism' where participants walk for the app/prize but revert to car use immediately after, with no mechanism to ensure sustained habit change.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on highly optimistic and unverified assumptions regarding government cooperation (e.g., forcing companies to allow home office, modifying bus routes) and the development of a custom Google app. The logistical requirement for 5% of a dense city's population to simultaneously walk or bike without infrastructure support presents significant safety and coordination risks that are not adequately addressed.

novelty.rationale: The core concept of 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days is a well-established global practice. While the proposal attempts to integrate a data-tracking app and specific local context (Buenos Aires), the fundamental mechanism and value proposition are not novel, merely a re-packaging of existing awareness campaigns.

impact.rationale: The proposal hides a critical normative trade-off: it frames the exclusion of public transit (buses/subways) as a climate solution, which is counter-productive and inequitable. By encouraging people to 'skip the bus' and 'avoid the subway,' the proposal risks increasing congestion for those who cannot walk or bike, while the claimed long-term impacts (e.g., 39 million tons of CO2 saved globally) are mathematically inflated and rely on speculative behavioral shifts that are not grounded in the one-day event's reality.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts to use data, but the presentation is marred by logical inconsistencies (e.g., calculating savings by avoiding public transport) and a lack of critical engagement with the exclusion risks of the proposed actions. The tone is optimistic but lacks the rigor required to justify the massive impact claims.

A5.perspective_summary: This proposal attempts to leverage a one-day event to drive systemic change but fails to address the distributional consequences of its core strategy. By explicitly advising participants to avoid public transit in favor of walking or biking, it inadvertently promotes a solution that is inaccessible to many (the elderly, disabled, or those with long commutes) and potentially harmful to the broader transit ecosystem. The value conflict between 'individual health/awareness' and 'collective transit efficiency' is ignored, leading to inflated impact metrics that do not reflect the true social cost of the proposed behavior change.
key_concerns: The proposal explicitly encourages skipping public transit ('skip the bus', 'avoid the subway'), which contradicts sustainable transport principles and excludes those who rely on it or cannot walk/bike.

The calculation of long-term GHG savings relies on speculative assumptions about policy changes (e.g., solar law approval, electric bus implementation) that are not direct results of a one-day walking event.
There is a significant risk of excluding low-income populations and people with disabilities who cannot walk or bike long distances, yet the proposal frames this as a universal solution.
The assumption that the government will mandate home office days or modify bus routes based on a single event is unrealistic and ignores the political and economic trade-offs involved.
The proposal lacks a clear mechanism for ensuring safety and accessibility for all participants, particularly in a high-density city like Buenos Aires.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a highly optimistic and unproven causal chain where a single-day awareness event triggers systemic policy shifts (e.g., 2% electric bus fleet conversion, 1% solar coverage) and behavioral changes (10% recycling increase) over five years. The assumption that a one-off 'Walk Day' will compel governments to restructure public transit routes or pass distributed solar laws lacks a credible implementation pathway. Furthermore, the reliance on a 'Google-developed' app and immediate government authorization for route modifications presents significant logistical and political hurdles that are not addressed.

novelty.rationale: The core concept of a 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' day is a well-established global practice (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work Day) and does not introduce new mechanisms for sustainability. While the proposal attempts to integrate 'Big Data' and specific local context (Buenos Aires' density), the fundamental approach of using a one-day event to drive long-term systemic change is a standard, low-novelty awareness tactic rather than an innovative intervention.

impact.rationale: The short-term impact is negligible (66 tCO2e/day) and transient, likely resulting in a 'rebound effect' where participants return to car usage immediately after the event. The long-term impact projections are speculative and mathematically disconnected from the intervention; attributing 35,700 tonnes of annual savings from electric buses or 70,000 tonnes from solar laws to a single walking event is a severe attribution error. The proposal fails to demonstrate how a temporary behavior change translates into the permanent structural resilience required for long-term ecological impact.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts to use data, but the presentation of the 'Long term impacts' is misleading. It presents highly uncertain policy outcomes (solar law approval, bus electrification) as direct, calculable results of the walking event, which obscures the actual causal mechanism. The inclusion of business model canvases and references to existing programs adds some professional polish, but the logical gaps in the impact narrative undermine the overall credibility.

A6.perspective_summary: From a long-term resilience perspective, this proposal confuses a temporary awareness spike with systemic transformation. While promoting walking has immediate health and minor emission benefits, the proposal's core flaw is the assumption that a single-day event can catalyze the complex, multi-year policy and infrastructure changes (solar laws, electric bus fleets) required for genuine long-term impact. It risks creating a 'feel-good' moment that shifts attention away from the harder, structural work needed for intergenerational sustainability.
key_concerns: Attribution Error: The proposal incorrectly attributes long-term infrastructure and policy changes (solar laws, electric bus adoption) to a single-day awareness event without a mechanism for how the event drives these specific legislative or capital investments.
Rebound Effect: There is no strategy to ensure the behavioral change (walking/biking) persists beyond the event day, raising the risk that the short-term emission savings are immediately negated.

Systemic Resilience: The proposal focuses on individual behavior change rather than building systemic resilience (e.g., urban planning, transit infrastructure) which is necessary for long-term adaptation to climate change.

Data Reliability: The reliance on a hypothetical app and the extrapolation of local data to global scales (1087 million people) without accounting for varying urban densities and transport cultures renders the long-term impact figures highly speculative.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal rests on highly fragile causal chains regarding institutional behavior. Specifically, the assumption that a government will re-route public transport for a single-day event and that a major tech entity (Google) will develop a custom app without a formal procurement framework represents a critical failure in operational planning. While the core activity (walking) is physically feasible, the systemic enablers required for the proposed scale (5% participation, route changes) lack empirical grounding in public administration realities.

novelty.rationale: The intervention itself—'Walk/Bike to Work' days—is a well-documented, mature practice globally. The proposal's only potential novelty lies in the specific integration of a tracking app to generate 'big data' for urban planning. However, the technical mechanism for this data collection (relying on a hypothetical Google app) is not novel, and the causal link between this data and the proposed long-term policy shifts (e.g., solar law approval) is speculative rather than innovative.

impact.rationale: The short-term emission savings (approx. 66 tCO2e/day) are mathematically plausible but negligible in the context of global or even city-wide emissions. The long-term impact projections (e.g., 39 million tCO2e/year globally) suffer from severe causal overreach; they assume a single-day behavioral nudge will directly trigger complex legislative and infrastructural changes (solar law, electric bus fleets) without detailing the necessary policy advocacy mechanisms. The extrapolation from a one-day event to systemic change lacks scientific plausibility.

presentation.rationale: The proposal attempts a structured approach with sections for costs, timeline, and impact modeling. However, the presentation is undermined by the inclusion of non-visible image references (e.g., 'Main value generation loops v1.0.png') and a logical disconnect between the event description and the impact calculations. The impact modeling conflates correlation with causation, presenting speculative policy outcomes as direct results of the event.

A1.perspective_summary: From a systems and empirical evidence perspective, this proposal is a classic 'awareness campaign' that significantly overestimates the causal power of a single event to drive systemic policy change. While the physical act of walking is low-risk, the proposal's feasibility is compromised by unrealistic dependencies on government and corporate behavior. The impact claims are scientifically weak, relying on linear extrapolations that ignore the complex, non-linear nature of policy adoption and infrastructure development.
key_concerns: The causal mechanism linking a one-day walking event to long-term legislative outcomes (e.g., solar law, recycling rates) is undefined and scientifically unsupported.
The proposal assumes a 'Google-developed' app will be created without a procurement process, creating a critical single point of failure.
The assumption that public transport routes will be modified for a single day contradicts standard operational constraints of transit authorities.
The 5% participation rate is an optimistic assumption lacking a validated incentive structure or marketing budget.
The long-term impact calculations appear to conflate independent policy trends with the specific effects of this event.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal faces severe institutional and regulatory hurdles. It assumes the government will modify public transport routes for a single day and that a major tech entity (Google) will develop a custom app, both of which are highly unrealistic without a formal, long-term public-private partnership framework. The reliance on a 'one-off' event to trigger complex legislative outcomes (solar law, electric bus fleet conversion) ignores the multi-year political cycles and bureaucratic inertia inherent in public governance. The lack of a defined budget or legal mechanism for data privacy and liability further reduces feasibility.

novelty.rationale: The concept of 'Walk/Bike to Work' days is a mature, globally standardized practice (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work Month). The proposed addition of a smartphone app for tracking is a common feature in existing commercial and municipal platforms (e.g., Strava, CityMapper) and does not constitute a novel governance mechanism or technological innovation. The proposal repackages existing awareness campaigns without introducing a new institutional model for behavior change.

impact.rationale: While the short-term emission savings are mathematically derived, the long-term impact projections suffer from a critical attribution error. The proposal incorrectly attributes systemic policy outcomes (e.g., 70,000+ tCO2e/year from solar law, 35,000+ tCO2e/year from electric buses) to a single-day awareness event. These outcomes depend on independent legislative and capital investment cycles, not the event itself. The governance capacity to translate a one-day behavioral nudge into permanent infrastructure change is not demonstrated, making the projected impact speculative and likely inflated.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts to provide quantitative backing, which is positive for transparency. However, the presentation is undermined by technical inconsistencies (typos, broken image references) and a lack of clarity in the causal logic connecting the event to long-term policy shifts. The data sources are cited, but the derivation of long-term figures conflates correlation with causation, reducing the scientific and policy credibility of the document.

A2.perspective_summary: From a governance and regulatory perspective, this proposal functions as a symbolic awareness campaign rather than a viable policy intervention. The institutional fit is weak, as it relies on unrealistic assumptions about government agility (route changes) and corporate altruism (custom app development) without a formal governance framework. The causal chain linking a one-day event to complex, multi-year legislative and infrastructure outcomes is tenuous and ignores the political and bureaucratic realities of public administration.
key_concerns: Institutional Feasibility: The assumption that the government will alter public transport routes for a single day lacks a legal or operational basis and ignores standard bureaucratic procedures.
Attribution Error: The proposal conflates a one-day behavioral nudge with systemic infrastructure changes (solar law, electric buses) that require independent legislative and capital cycles.
Governance Capacity: There is no defined mechanism for data privacy, liability, or public accountability regarding the proposed app and the collection of citizen movement data.
Resource Dependency: The plan relies on unsecured sponsorships and the voluntary cooperation of major tech giants (Google) without a formal procurement or partnership strategy.
Scalability: The extrapolation to a global scale assumes uniform political will and behavioral response across diverse geopolitical contexts, which is not supported by governance evidence.

feasibility.rationale: Operational feasibility remains critically low. The proposal assumes a 'Google-developed' app will be instantly available and free, ignoring the massive development, maintenance, and data privacy costs. Furthermore, the requirement for the government to modify public transport routes for a single day and for companies to mandate home office days represents a coordination nightmare with no clear incentive structure for these stakeholders. As noted by peers, the assumption that a one-day event triggers multi-year legislative changes (solar law, electric bus fleets) is operationally impossible without a dedicated, long-term advocacy apparatus, which is absent here.

novelty.rationale: The concept is a repackaging of established 'Bike to Work' and 'Walk to Work' campaigns. The proposed 'innovation'—a tracking app and a link to long-term policy—is not novel; similar gamification exists in apps like Strava or Citymapper, and the causal link between a single event and systemic policy shifts is a logical fallacy rather than a novel mechanism. The proposal adds no new operational model or incentive structure that distinguishes it from decades of similar awareness campaigns.

impact.rationale: While the short-term emission savings (approx. 66 tCO2e) are mathematically derived, they are negligible in the context of global or even city-wide emissions. The long-term impact projections are fundamentally flawed from an implementation standpoint: attributing the approval of a solar law or the deployment of electric buses to a single walking event is a severe attribution error. Real-world scaling of such events rarely results in the systemic infrastructure changes proposed without a dedicated, multi-year policy campaign, which this proposal does not outline.

presentation.rationale: The proposal attempts a structured approach with a Business Model Canvas and quantitative claims, which is a positive step. However, the presentation is undermined by broken image references, typos, and a lack of clarity in the causal logic. The data sources are cited but the derivation of the massive long-term impact figures is opaque and scientifically unsound, reducing the credibility of the document for an implementation-focused review.

A3.perspective_summary: From an operational and implementation lens, this proposal is a high-risk, low-feasibility initiative. It relies on unrealistic assumptions about stakeholder coordination (government, Google, corporations) and conflates a symbolic one-day event with complex, capital-intensive infrastructure projects. While the intent to raise awareness is valid, the proposed mechanism lacks the operational rigor, resource planning, and realistic causal pathways required for actual deployment or scaling. The peers' identification of the 'attribution error' regarding long-term policy confirms that the impact model is not grounded in implementation reality.
key_concerns: Resource Gap: No budget or resource plan for app development, data privacy compliance, or event security/medical support.
Stakeholder Incentives: No clear value proposition for the government to alter transport routes or for companies to enforce home office days.
Causal Fallacy: The proposal incorrectly assumes a single event can drive independent legislative and capital investment cycles (solar law, electric buses).
Scalability Barrier: The model relies on a 'copy-paste' approach to global scaling without accounting for local regulatory, cultural, or infrastructural differences.
Data Integrity: Reliance on undefined algorithms and broken visual aids prevents verification of the proposed value loops.

feasibility.rationale: While the idea of a community walk is simple, the proposal's feasibility collapses when viewed from the ground. The requirement to 'skip the bus' and 'avoid the subway' is not just logistically difficult for those with long commutes or mobility issues; it is socially impossible for the working class in Buenos Aires who rely on these systems to get to work. Furthermore, the assumption that the government will mandate home office days or that a single event will trigger complex infrastructure changes (like bus route modifications) ignores the daily reality of labor rights and bureaucratic inertia. As peers A5 and A6 noted, the reliance on a custom Google app and top-down mandates makes this unworkable without massive, unverified political capital.

novelty.rationale: The core concept is a standard 'Walk to Work' day, which is a well-known global practice. The proposal attempts to add novelty through a 'Big Data' app and specific local context, but the fundamental mechanism remains a one-off awareness event. From a community perspective, this feels like a re-packaging of existing campaigns that often fail to engage the most vulnerable populations because they don't address the structural barriers (distance, safety, disability) that prevent people from walking or biking.

impact.rationale: The proposed impact is deeply flawed because it explicitly encourages excluding public transit, which is the most equitable and low-carbon mode of transport for the majority. By telling people to 'skip the bus,' the proposal risks increasing congestion for those who cannot walk, while the claimed long-term impacts (e.g., 39 million tons of CO2 saved globally) are mathematically inflated and rely on speculative behavioral shifts that are not grounded in the reality of a one-day event. The peers correctly identified that attributing systemic policy changes (solar laws, electric buses) to a single walking day is a severe attribution error that misleads stakeholders about the true potential of the intervention.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts to use data, but the presentation is marred by logical inconsistencies and a lack of critical engagement with the exclusion risks. The tone is optimistic but lacks the rigor required to justify the massive impact claims. The inclusion of business model canvases and references to existing programs adds some professional polish, but the logical gaps in the impact narrative, particularly the exclusion of public transit, undermine the overall credibility from a community perspective.

A4.perspective_summary: From the ground, this proposal feels like a well-intentioned but disconnected exercise that ignores the daily realities of the people it aims to serve. The explicit instruction to avoid public transit is a major red flag, as it excludes the elderly, disabled, and low-income residents who rely on buses and subways. The proposal's reliance on a single-day event to drive systemic change is unrealistic, and the inflated impact metrics fail to account for the social and logistical barriers that prevent widespread participation. The peers' critiques highlight the critical flaws in the proposal's logic and feasibility, reinforcing the need for a more grounded and inclusive approach.
key_concerns: The proposal explicitly encourages skipping public transit ('skip the bus', 'avoid the subway'), which contradicts sustainable transport principles and excludes those who rely on it or cannot walk/bike.

The calculation of long-term GHG savings relies on speculative assumptions about policy changes (e.g., solar law approval, electric bus implementation) that are not direct results of a one-day walking event.
There is a significant risk of excluding low-income populations and people with disabilities who cannot walk or bike long distances, yet the proposal frames this as a universal solution.
The assumption that the government will mandate home office days or modify bus routes based on a single event is unrealistic and ignores the political and economic trade-offs involved.
The proposal lacks a clear mechanism for ensuring safety and accessibility for all participants, particularly in a high-density city like Buenos Aires.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal's feasibility is critically undermined by its reliance on top-down mandates that ignore the distributional realities of the workforce. The assumption that the government will modify transit routes and that companies will universally allow home office for a single day fails to account for the exclusion of essential workers, low-income employees, and those in informal sectors who cannot 'just walk' or work remotely. As peers A4 and A6 noted, the logistical barriers are high, but from a justice lens, the plan is infeasible because it presumes a level of privilege (time, mobility, remote work capability) that is not universally shared, risking the exclusion of the very populations most vulnerable to climate impacts.

novelty.rationale: The concept of 'Walk to Work' days is a well-established global practice with no novel mechanisms for addressing systemic inequity. The proposal attempts to add value through 'big data' collection, but this does not constitute innovation in the realm of justice or inclusion; rather, it risks commodifying the movement of marginalized bodies for corporate or state data extraction without clear benefits returning to those communities. The approach remains a standard awareness campaign that does not challenge existing power structures or offer new pathways for inclusive climate action.

impact.rationale: The projected impact is heavily skewed toward the benefits of the privileged (health, cost savings for those who can walk/work from home) while ignoring the costs borne by those excluded. The proposal claims long-term systemic changes (solar laws, electric buses) as direct results of a one-day event, which is a severe attribution error that hides the reality that structural change requires sustained political pressure, not just a 'feel-good' event. Furthermore, the 'impact' calculation assumes a 5% participation rate that likely excludes the poor, the disabled, and the elderly, meaning the benefits are concentrated among the able-bodied and employed, while the 'cost' of reduced transit efficiency (if routes are modified) falls on those who rely on public transport for survival.

presentation.rationale: The presentation is structured but lacks a critical analysis of who is left out. It presents optimistic, aggregate numbers (e.g., 39 million tCO2e/year globally) without addressing the normative trade-offs or the exclusion risks inherent in the proposed actions. The reliance on image placeholders and the omission of a discussion on accessibility or the needs of non-car owners creates a narrative that is incomplete and potentially misleading regarding the true scope of the proposal's justice implications.

A5.perspective_summary: This proposal presents a classic case of 'greenwashing' through individual behavior change that ignores structural inequities. While the intent to raise awareness is noble, the execution plan assumes a level of privilege and institutional cooperation that does not exist, effectively excluding the most vulnerable populations. The 'big data' component risks turning participants into data points for corporate gain without ensuring that the resulting insights lead to equitable policy changes. The proposal fails to address the fundamental question: who bears the cost of this event, and who is excluded from its benefits?
key_concerns: Exclusion of essential workers, low-income individuals, and people with disabilities who cannot walk to work or work from home.
Risk of 'performative activism' where the event benefits the privileged while failing to address the systemic barriers to sustainable transport for the poor.
The 'big data' collection raises ethical concerns about privacy and the potential for data to be used for corporate marketing rather than public good.
The proposal attributes long-term structural changes (solar laws, electric buses) to a single-day event, obscuring the need for sustained, inclusive political advocacy.
Lack of a mechanism to ensure that the benefits of the event (e.g., improved transit, health) are equitably distributed across all socioeconomic groups.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal's feasibility is critically undermined by its reliance on a one-day behavioral shift to trigger complex, long-term systemic changes (e.g., new solar laws, electric bus fleets) that typically require decades of policy work. As peers A4 and A5 noted, the assumption that a single event will compel governments to modify transit routes or mandate home office is operationally fragile. From a resilience perspective, building a strategy on such volatile, high-level political dependencies rather than incremental, community-led infrastructure improvements makes the project highly susceptible to failure.

novelty.rationale: The concept of 'Walk to Work' days is a well-established global practice with limited novelty. The proposal attempts to add value through 'big data' tracking, but this does not fundamentally alter the systemic nature of the intervention. The peers correctly identified that the core mechanism is a standard awareness campaign. While the specific local context (Buenos Aires) is relevant, the approach does not offer a new model for long-term societal resilience or ecological adaptation.

impact.rationale: The long-term ecological and societal impact is likely negligible and potentially counter-productive. The proposal explicitly encourages 'skipping the bus' and 'avoiding the subway,' which contradicts the principles of sustainable urban mobility and reduces the efficiency of the public transit system—a critical resilience asset. As peers A4 and A5 highlighted, the extrapolation of a single day's data to predict global policy shifts (e.g., 39 million tons of CO2 saved) is mathematically unsound and ignores the second-order effects of reducing public transit ridership. True long-term impact requires shifting to *more* efficient public transport, not away from it.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections but suffers from logical inconsistencies regarding the primary action (walking vs. public transit) and relies on image placeholders that obscure the argument. The optimistic tone masks a lack of grounded detail on how the proposed 'value generation loops' actually function in a complex urban system. The presentation fails to address the systemic risks identified by peers, such as accessibility and the potential erosion of public transit viability.

A6.perspective_summary: From a long-term sustainability and resilience lens, this proposal presents a 'shallow' intervention that risks undermining the very systems (public transit) needed for deep decarbonization. By framing the avoidance of public transport as a climate solution, it ignores the systemic necessity of high-capacity, low-carbon mobility. The reliance on a single event to trigger decades of policy change reflects a misunderstanding of how societal resilience is built—through gradual, structural adaptation rather than episodic awareness. The potential for 'performative activism' without lasting behavioral change is high, and the exclusion of non-walkable populations threatens social equity.
key_concerns: The proposal's directive to 'skip the bus' and 'avoid the subway' actively degrades the utility and ridership of public transit, a critical infrastructure for long-term urban resilience and equity.
The causal link between a one-day walking event and major policy shifts (solar laws, electric bus fleets) is tenuous and ignores the complex, multi-decadal nature of systemic change.
The exclusion of populations with mobility impairments, the elderly, and those with long commutes creates a social equity gap that undermines the proposal's claim to be a universal solution.
The 'big data' collection strategy relies on a custom app that may not be scalable or trusted, creating a fragile data dependency rather than a robust community feedback loop.

The proposal fails to address the second-order effects of reduced public transit usage, such as increased congestion for those who cannot walk, potentially worsening local air quality and social friction.

feasibility.rationale: All three experts (A1, A2, A3) unanimously assigned a score of 2, citing critical operational failures. The proposal relies on unrealistic dependencies, specifically the assumption that a major tech entity (Google) will develop a custom app without procurement and that a government will alter public transport routes for a single day. The lack of a defined budget, legal framework for data privacy, or incentive structure for corporate participation renders the operational plan unfeasible.

novelty.rationale: Experts A2 and A3 assigned a score of 2, while A1 assigned 3. The consensus view is that 'Walk/Bike to Work' is a mature, globally standardized practice. The proposed 'innovation' of using a tracking app is not novel, as similar gamification exists in commercial platforms (e.g., Strava). The divergence was resolved by adopting the lower score, as the proposal fails to introduce a new institutional model or technological mechanism that distinguishes it from decades of existing awareness campaigns.

impact.rationale: All experts assigned a score of 2. While the short-term emission savings are mathematically derived, the long-term impact projections are fundamentally flawed due to severe attribution errors. The proposal incorrectly attributes complex, multi-year legislative and infrastructure outcomes (e.g., solar law approval, electric bus fleet conversion) to a single-day behavioral nudge. The causal chain linking the event to systemic policy change is undefined and scientifically unsupported.

presentation.rationale: All experts assigned a score of 3. The proposal attempts a structured approach with sections for costs, timeline, and impact modeling, which provides a baseline of transparency. However, the presentation is significantly undermined by broken image references, typos, and a logical disconnect between the event description and the speculative impact calculations. The inclusion of non-visible assets and opaque data derivations reduces the document's scientific credibility.

S1.synthesis: The cluster's judgment is highly convergent. All three experts identified the same core pathologies: a disconnect between the symbolic nature of a one-day event and the massive systemic changes it claims to trigger. The synthesis integrates the operational concerns (A3), governance/regulatory hurdles (A2), and systems/evidence perspective (A1) to conclude that while the intent is positive, the mechanism is structurally unsound. The proposal is viewed as a classic 'awareness campaign' that overestimates its causal power and ignores the bureaucratic and political realities required to achieve its stated long-term goals.
S1.uncertainty: The primary uncertainty lies in the potential for the proposal to be re-scoped as a purely symbolic awareness event without the inflated long-term impact claims. If the proposal were revised to remove the attribution of legislative outcomes (solar law, electric buses) to the event and instead focus solely on the immediate behavioral shift and data collection for future advocacy, the feasibility and impact scores could improve. Currently, the judgment is robust regarding the impossibility of the current causal model, but the potential for a revised, more modest version of the project remains unassessed.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal is deemed unfeasible due to its reliance on unrealistic top-down mandates (e.g., government-mandated home office, bus route modifications) and a core directive to 'skip the bus' and 'avoid the subway.' This contradicts the daily reality of the working class in Buenos Aires, who depend on public transit, and ignores the logistical impossibility of walking long commutes or for those with mobility issues. The consensus across all agents is that the project fails to account for structural barriers and labor rights.

novelty.rationale: The concept is a standard 'Walk to Work' awareness campaign, a well-established global practice with no novel mechanisms for addressing systemic inequity. While the proposal attempts to differentiate itself through 'Big Data' tracking and a smartphone app, agents agree this adds little substantive innovation and risks commodifying participant movement without offering new pathways for inclusive climate action or challenging existing power structures.

impact.rationale: The projected impact is critically flawed. The proposal explicitly encourages reducing public transit usage, which undermines the most equitable and low-carbon mode of transport. Furthermore, the long-term impact calculations (e.g., 39 million tons of CO2 saved globally) are mathematically inflated and rely on severe attribution errors, assuming a single-day event will trigger complex, decades-long policy changes like solar law approvals or electric bus fleet overhauls. The benefits are skewed toward the privileged, while the costs fall on those excluded from the event.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structurally organized with clear sections, business model canvases, and data references, earning a moderate score. However, the presentation is marred by logical inconsistencies, particularly the contradiction between promoting walking and discouraging public transit. It lacks critical engagement with exclusion risks and relies on optimistic, aggregate numbers that obscure the normative trade-offs and accessibility gaps inherent in the plan.

S2.synthesis: The cluster (A4, A5, A6) reached a strong consensus that the proposal, while well-intentioned, is fundamentally disconnected from the socio-economic and logistical realities of its target environment. A4 highlighted the ground-level impossibility of skipping public transit for the working class; A5 emphasized the justice lens, noting the exclusion of essential workers and the risk of 'greenwashing' through individual behavior change; and A6 focused on the resilience perspective, arguing that the proposal undermines the public transit system needed for deep decarbonization. These perspectives were integrated to form a unified view: the proposal's core mechanism (avoiding transit) is counter-productive to climate goals and social equity. The scores were harmonized at 2 across feasibility, novelty, and impact, reflecting a shared view that the project is unworkable and potentially harmful in its current form, while presentation received a 3 for its structural clarity despite its logical flaws.
S2.uncertainty: The primary uncertainty lies in the potential for the proposal to be re-scoped. If the directive to 'skip the bus' were removed and the focus shifted to 'walking the last mile' or integrating with public transit rather than replacing it, the feasibility and impact scores could improve significantly. Additionally, the specific scalability of the 'Big Data' app and its ability to genuinely influence policy (rather than just serving as a marketing tool) remains an open question that depends on future implementation details not present in the current text.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal is deemed unfeasible due to critical operational and structural failures. Both clusters agree on unrealistic dependencies, such as expecting Google to develop a custom app without procurement and the government to alter transit routes for a single day. Furthermore, the directive to 'skip the bus' and 'avoid the subway' contradicts the logistical reality of the working class in Buenos Aires, making the core action impossible for a significant portion of the target population.

novelty.rationale: The concept is a mature, globally standardized 'Walk/Bike to Work' campaign. The proposed use of a tracking app and 'Big Data' does not constitute a novel mechanism, as similar gamification exists in commercial platforms (e.g., Strava). The proposal fails to introduce a new institutional model or technological approach that distinguishes it from decades of existing awareness efforts.

impact.rationale: The projected impact is fundamentally flawed due to severe attribution errors. The proposal incorrectly attributes complex, multi-year legislative and infrastructure outcomes (e.g., solar law approval, electric bus fleet conversion) to a single-day behavioral nudge. Additionally, the core directive to avoid public transit undermines the most equitable and low-carbon mode of transport, potentially causing net negative social and environmental outcomes despite the short-term emission math.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structurally organized with clear sections, business model canvases, and data references, earning a moderate score. However, the presentation is significantly undermined by broken image references, typos, and a logical disconnect between the event description and the speculative impact calculations. The inclusion of non-visible assets and opaque data derivations reduces the document's scientific credibility.

final.synthesis: The final judgment integrates the operational and governance concerns from Cluster S1 (A1-A3) with the socio-economic and equity perspectives from Cluster S2 (A4-A6). Both clusters converged on low scores for feasibility, novelty, and impact, though for slightly different emphases: S1 focused on the bureaucratic impossibility and lack of causal logic, while S2 highlighted the social injustice of discouraging public transit and the exclusion of essential workers. The synthesis confirms that the proposal is structurally unsound; it relies on a 'top-down' mandate that ignores ground-level realities and overestimates the causal power of a one-day event to trigger systemic policy change. The presentation score reflects the effort in structure but penalizes the logical inconsistencies and lack of polish.
final.uncertainty: The primary uncertainty lies in the potential for the proposal to be re-scoped. If the directive to 'skip the bus' were removed and the focus shifted to 'walking the last mile' or integrating with public transit rather than replacing it, the feasibility and impact scores could improve significantly. Additionally, the specific scalability of the 'Big Data' app and its ability to genuinely influence policy (rather than just serving as a marketing tool) remains an open question that depends on future implementation details not present in the current text. The current judgment assumes the proposal remains as written, where the trade-off between 'awareness' and 'systemic impact' is heavily skewed toward the latter without the necessary mechanisms to support it.
